tools.description: Create a new GitHub issue for tracking bugs, feature requests, or tasks. Use this for actionable work items that need assignment, labeling, and status tracking. For reports, announcements, or status updates that don't require task tracking, use create_discussion instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 1 issue(s) can be created.

body.description: Detailed issue description in Markdown. Do NOT repeat the title as a heading since it already appears as the issue's h1. Include context, reproduction steps, or acceptance criteria as appropriate.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

labels.description: Labels to categorize the issue (e.g., 'bug', 'enhancement'). Labels must exist in the repository.

parent.description: Parent issue number for creating sub-issues. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 42 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/42). Can also be a temporary_id (e.g., 'aw_abc123', 'aw_Test123') from a previously created issue in the same workflow run.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

temporary_id.description: Unique temporary identifier for referencing this issue before it's created. Format: 'aw_' followed by 3 to 12 alphanumeric characters (e.g., 'aw_abc1', 'aw_Test123'). Use '#aw_ID' in body text to reference other issues by their temporary_id; these are replaced with actual issue numbers after creation.

title.description: Concise issue title summarizing the bug, feature, or task. The title appears as the main heading, so keep it brief and descriptive.

tools.name: create_issue

tools.description: Report that a tool or capability needed to complete the task is not available, or share any information you deem important about missing functionality or limitations. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because the required functionality is missing or access is restricted.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this tool is needed or what information you want to share about the limitation (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tool.description: Optional: Name or description of the missing tool or capability (max 128 characters). Be specific about what functionality is needed.

tools.name: missing_tool

tools.description: Log a transparency message when no significant actions are needed. Use this to confirm workflow completion and provide visibility when analysis is complete but no changes or outputs are required (e.g., 'No issues found', 'All checks passed'). This ensures the workflow produces human-visible output even when no other actions are taken.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

message.description: Status or completion message to log. Should explain what was analyzed and the outcome (e.g., 'Code review complete - no issues found', 'Analysis complete - all tests passing').

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: noop

tools.description: Report that data or information needed to complete the task is not available. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because required data, context, or information is missing.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

context.description: Additional context about the missing data or where it should come from (max 256 characters).

data_type.description: Type or description of the missing data or information (max 128 characters). Be specific about what data is needed.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this data is needed to complete the task (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: missing_data

tools.description: Signal that the task could not be completed due to an infrastructure or tool failure (e.g., MCP server crash, missing authentication, inaccessible repository). Use this when required tools or data are unavailable and the task cannot be meaningfully performed. This is distinct from noop (no action needed) — it indicates an active failure that prevented the task from running. The workflow framework will treat this as a failure signal even when the agent exits successfully.

details.description: Optional extended details or diagnostic context about the failure (max 65000 characters).

reason.description: A concise explanation of why the task could not be completed (max 1024 characters). Be specific about which tools or resources were unavailable.

tools.name: report_incomplete

tools.description: Dispatch the issue triage workflow for the newly created issue
tools.name: dispatch_triage

tools.description: Get details for a commit from a GitHub repository

include_diff.description: Whether to include file diffs and stats in the response. Default is true.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

sha.description: Commit SHA, branch name, or tag name

tools.name: get_commit

tools.description: Get the contents of a file or directory from a GitHub repository

ref.description: Accepts optional git refs such as `refs/tags/{tag}`, `refs/heads/{branch}` or `refs/pull/{pr_number}/head`

sha.description: Accepts optional commit SHA. If specified, it will be used instead of ref

tools.name: get_file_contents

annotations.title: Get a specific label from a repository.

tools.description: Get a specific label from a repository.
tools.name: get_label

tools.description: Get the latest release in a GitHub repository
tools.name: get_latest_release

annotations.title: Get a release by tag name

tools.description: Get a specific release by its tag name in a GitHub repository
tools.name: get_release_by_tag

tools.description: Get details about a specific git tag in a GitHub repository
tools.name: get_tag

tools.description: Get information about a specific issue in a GitHub repository.

method.description:
The read operation to perform on a single issue.
Options are:
1. get - Get details of a specific issue.
2. get_comments - Get issue comments.
3. get_sub_issues - Get sub-issues of the issue.
4. get_labels - Get labels assigned to the issue.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: issue_read

tools.description: List branches in a GitHub repository

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: list_branches

tools.description: Get list of commits of a branch in a GitHub repository. Returns at least 30 results per page by default, but can return more if specified using the perPage parameter (up to 100).

author.description: Author username or email address to filter commits by

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

sha.description: Commit SHA, branch or tag name to list commits of. If not provided, uses the default branch of the repository. If a commit SHA is provided, will list commits up to that SHA.

tools.name: list_commits

tools.description: List supported issue types for repository owner (organization).

owner.description: The organization owner of the repository

tools.name: list_issue_types

tools.description: List issues in a GitHub repository. For pagination, use the 'endCursor' from the previous response's 'pageInfo' in the 'after' parameter.

after.description: Cursor for pagination. Use the endCursor from the previous page's PageInfo for GraphQL APIs.

direction.description: Order direction. If provided, the 'orderBy' also needs to be provided.

orderBy.description: Order issues by field. If provided, the 'direction' also needs to be provided.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

state.description: Filter by state, by default both open and closed issues are returned when not provided

tools.name: list_issues

tools.description: List releases in a GitHub repository

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: list_releases

tools.description: List git tags in a GitHub repository

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: list_tags

tools.description: Fast and precise code search across ALL GitHub repositories using GitHub's native search engine. Best for finding exact symbols, functions, classes, or specific code patterns.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Search query using GitHub's powerful code search syntax. Examples: 'content:Skill language:Java org:github', 'NOT is:archived language:Python OR language:go', 'repo:github/github-mcp-server'. Supports exact matching, language filters, path filters, and more.

tools.name: search_code

tools.description: Search for issues in GitHub repositories using issues search syntax already scoped to is:issue

owner.description: Optional repository owner. If provided with repo, only issues for this repository are listed.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Search query using GitHub issues search syntax

repo.description: Optional repository name. If provided with owner, only issues for this repository are listed.

sort.description: Sort field by number of matches of categories, defaults to best match

tools.name: search_issues

tools.description: Find GitHub repositories by name, description, readme, topics, or other metadata. Perfect for discovering projects, finding examples, or locating specific repositories across GitHub.

minimal_output.description: Return minimal repository information (default: true). When false, returns full GitHub API repository objects.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Repository search query. Examples: 'machine learning in:name stars:>1000 language:python', 'topic:react', 'user:facebook'. Supports advanced search syntax for precise filtering.

sort.description: Sort repositories by field, defaults to best match

tools.name: search_repositories

# Release History

## 1.0.0-beta.2 (Unreleased)

### Features Added

### Breaking Changes

### Bugs Fixed

### Other Changes

## 1.0.0-beta.1 (2026-04-16)

### Features Added

- Initial beta release of Azure.Provisioning.ResourceGraph with support for declarative Resource Graph resource provisioning.
- Added support for Resource Graph Query resources.

# Microsoft Azure Advisor management client library for .NET

Microsoft Advisor is a personalized cloud consultant that helps you follow best practices to optimize your Azure deployments. It analyzes your resource configuration and usage telemetry and then recommends solutions that can help you improve the cost effectiveness, performance, Reliability (formerly called High availability), and security of your Azure resources.

This library supports managing Microsoft Azure Advisor Configuration resources.

This library follows the [new Azure SDK guidelines](https://azure.github.io/azure-sdk/general_introduction.html), and provides many core capabilities:

    - Support MSAL.NET, Azure.Identity is out of box for supporting MSAL.NET.
    - Support [OpenTelemetry](https://opentelemetry.io/) for distributed tracing.
    - HTTP pipeline with custom policies.
    - Better error-handling.
    - Support uniform telemetry across all languages.

## Getting started 

### Install the package

Install the Microsoft Azure Advisor management library for .NET with [NuGet](https://www.nuget.org/):

```dotnetcli
dotnet add package Azure.ResourceManager.Advisor --prerelease
```

### Prerequisites

* You must have an [Microsoft Azure subscription](https://azure.microsoft.com/free/dotnet/).

### Authenticate the Client

To create an authenticated client and start interacting with Microsoft Azure resources, see the [quickstart guide here](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/doc/dev/mgmt_quickstart.md).

## Key concepts

Key concepts of the Microsoft Azure SDK for .NET can be found [here](https://azure.github.io/azure-sdk/dotnet_introduction.html).

## Documentation

Documentation is available to help you learn how to use this package:

- [Quickstart](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/doc/dev/mgmt_quickstart.md).
- [API References](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/?view=azure-dotnet).
- [Authentication](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md).

## Examples

Code samples for using the management library for .NET can be found in the following locations
- [.NET Management Library Code Samples](https://aka.ms/azuresdk-net-mgmt-samples)

## Troubleshooting

-   File an issue via [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues).
-   Check [previous questions](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/azure+.net) or ask new ones on Stack Overflow using Azure and .NET tags.

## Next steps

For more information about Microsoft Azure SDK, see [this website](https://azure.github.io/azure-sdk/).

## Contributing

For details on contributing to this repository, see the [contributing
guide][cg].

This project welcomes contributions and suggestions. Most contributions
require you to agree to a Contributor License Agreement (CLA) declaring
that you have the right to, and actually do, grant us the rights to use
your contribution. For details, visit <https://cla.microsoft.com>.

When you submit a pull request, a CLA-bot will automatically determine
whether you need to provide a CLA and decorate the PR appropriately
(for example, label, comment). Follow the instructions provided by the
bot. You'll only need to do this action once across all repositories
using our CLA.

This project has adopted the [Microsoft Open Source Code of Conduct][coc]. For
more information, see the [Code of Conduct FAQ][coc_faq] or contact
<opencode@microsoft.com> with any other questions or comments.

<!-- LINKS -->
[cg]: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/resourcemanager/Azure.ResourceManager/docs/CONTRIBUTING.md
[coc]: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/
[coc_faq]: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/faq/

# Release History

## 1.0.0-beta.7 (Unreleased)

### Features Added

### Breaking Changes

### Bugs Fixed

### Other Changes

## 1.0.0-beta.6 (2026-04-17)

### Features Added

Upgraded api-version to 2025-05-01-preview.

### Other Changes

- Upgraded dependent `Azure.Core` to `1.53.0`.
- Upgraded dependent `Azure.ResourceManager` to `1.14.0`.

## 1.0.0-beta.5 (2025-03-11)

### Features Added

- Enable the new model serialization by using the System.ClientModel, refer this [document](https://aka.ms/azsdk/net/mrw) for more details.
- Exposed `JsonModelWriteCore` for model serialization procedure.

## 1.0.0-beta.4 (2023-11-27)

### Features Added

- Enable mocking for extension methods, refer this [document](https://aka.ms/azsdk/net/mocking) for more details.

### Other Changes

- Upgraded dependent `Azure.ResourceManager` to 1.9.0.

## 1.0.0-beta.3 (2023-05-25)

### Features Added

- Enable the model factory feature for model mocking, more information can be found [here](https://azure.github.io/azure-sdk/dotnet_introduction.html#dotnet-mocking-factory-builder).

### Other Changes

- Upgraded dependent Azure.Core to 1.32.0.
- Upgraded dependent Azure.ResourceManager to 1.6.0.

## 1.0.0-beta.2 (2023-02-16)

### Breaking Changes

- Rename some extensions methods

### Other Changes

- Upgraded dependent `Azure.Core` to `1.28.0`.
- Upgraded dependent `Azure.ResourceManager` to `1.4.0`.

## 1.0.0-beta.1 (2022-09-14)

### Breaking Changes

New design of track 2 initial commit.

### Package Name

The package name has been changed from `Microsoft.Azure.Management.Advisor` to `Azure.ResourceManager.Advisor`.

### General New Features

This package follows the [new Azure SDK guidelines](https://azure.github.io/azure-sdk/general_introduction.html), and provides many core capabilities:

    - Support MSAL.NET, Azure.Identity is out of box for supporting MSAL.NET.
    - Support [OpenTelemetry](https://opentelemetry.io/) for distributed tracing.
    - HTTP pipeline with custom policies.
    - Better error-handling.
    - Support uniform telemetry across all languages.

This package is a Public Preview version, so expect incompatible changes in subsequent releases as we improve the product. To provide feedback, submit an issue in our [Azure SDK for .NET GitHub repo](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues).

> NOTE: For more information about unified authentication, please refer to [Microsoft Azure Identity documentation for .NET](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/overview/azure/identity-readme?view=azure-dotnet).

# Azure Provisioning ResourceGraph client library for .NET

Azure.Provisioning.ResourceGraph simplifies declarative resource provisioning for Azure Resource Graph in .NET.

## Getting started

### Install the package

Install the client library for .NET with [NuGet](https://www.nuget.org/):

```dotnetcli
dotnet add package Azure.Provisioning.ResourceGraph --prerelease
```

### Prerequisites

> You must have an [Azure subscription](https://azure.microsoft.com/free/dotnet/).

### Authenticate the Client

## Key concepts

This library allows you to specify your infrastructure in a declarative style using dotnet.  You can then use `azd` to deploy your infrastructure to Azure directly without needing to write or maintain bicep or arm templates.

## Examples

### Create a Resource Graph Shared Query

This example demonstrates how to create a Resource Graph shared query that counts virtual machines by OS type, based on the [Azure quickstart template](https://github.com/Azure/azure-quickstart-templates/tree/master/demos/resourcegraph-sharedquery-countos).

```C# Snippet:ResourceGraphQueryBasic
Infrastructure infra = new();

ProvisioningParameter queryCode = new(nameof(queryCode), typeof(string))
{
    Description = "The Azure Resource Graph query to be saved to the shared query.",
    Value = "Resources | where type =~ 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines' | summarize count() by tostring(properties.storageProfile.osDisk.osType)",
};
infra.Add(queryCode);

ProvisioningParameter queryDescription = new(nameof(queryDescription), typeof(string))
{
    Description = "The description of the saved Azure Resource Graph query.",
    Value = "This shared query counts all virtual machine resources and summarizes by the OS type.",
};
infra.Add(queryDescription);

ResourceGraphQuery query =
    new(nameof(query))
    {
        Query = queryCode,
        Description = queryDescription,
    };
infra.Add(query);
```

## Troubleshooting

-   File an issue via [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues).
-   Check [previous questions](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/azure+.net) or ask new ones on Stack Overflow using Azure and .NET tags.

## Next steps

## Contributing

For details on contributing to this repository, see the [contributing
guide][cg].

This project welcomes contributions and suggestions. Most contributions
require you to agree to a Contributor License Agreement (CLA) declaring
that you have the right to, and actually do, grant us the rights to use
your contribution. For details, visit <https://cla.microsoft.com>.

When you submit a pull request, a CLA-bot will automatically determine
whether you need to provide a CLA and decorate the PR appropriately
(for example, label, comment). Follow the instructions provided by the
bot. You'll only need to do this action once across all repositories
using our CLA.

This project has adopted the [Microsoft Open Source Code of Conduct][coc]. For
more information, see the [Code of Conduct FAQ][coc_faq] or contact
<opencode@microsoft.com> with any other questions or comments.

<!-- LINKS -->
[cg]: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/resourcemanager/Azure.ResourceManager/docs/CONTRIBUTING.md
[coc]: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/
[coc_faq]: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/faq/

arguments.body:
## Documentation Gap **Package:** `Azure.Provisioning.ResourceGraph` **Service directory:** `sdk/resourcegraph/Azure.Provisioning.ResourceGraph/` **Triggered by:** commit [REDACTED] (post-release version bump for PR #58257) by @azure-sdk ## What Changed Initial public release (1.0.0-beta.1) of `Azure.Provisioning.ResourceGraph`, which enables declarative provisioning of Azure Resource Graph shared queries using the Azure Provisioning library pattern. The package exposes the `ResourceGraphQuery` resource type backed by `Microsoft.ResourceGraph/queries@2024-04-01`. ## Gaps Found - **Empty "Authenticate the Client" section**: The `### Authenticate the Client` sub-heading under "Getting started" exists but contains zero content. Consumers need guidance on what credentials to supply when using `azd` or the Azure Provisioning infrastructure APIs. - **Empty "Next steps" section**: The `## Next steps` heading exists but has no content — no links to API reference, related provisioning packages (e.g., `Azure.Provisioning.Resources`), or the `azd` deployment workflow. - **`FromExisting` pattern is undocumented**: `ResourceGraphQuery.FromExisting(...)` allows referencing a pre-existing Resource Graph query in a larger infrastructure definition, which is a key Provisioning library pattern. This method is not mentioned anywhere in the README. - **Output properties not explained**: The read-only output properties `ResultKind`, `ModifiedOn`, and `Id` can be referenced in downstream Bicep expressions. The README has no mention of reading outputs or using them in multi-resource configurations.
📐 Implementation Guide This section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above. ### Step 1: Modify files **File:** `sdk/resourcegraph/Azure.Provisioning.ResourceGraph/README.md` — **edit** 1. Fill in the empty `### Authenticate the Client` section (after the `### Prerequisites` section): ```markdown ### Authenticate the Client `Azure.Provisioning.ResourceGraph` is a declarative provisioning library — it generates Bicep/ARM templates rather than calling Azure APIs directly at runtime. Authentication is handled by the deployment tooling: - When using **Azure Developer CLI (`azd`)**, run `azd auth login` to authenticate before deploying. - When deploying programmatically, provide an `ArmClient` with your preferred [`TokenCredential`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md) (e.g., `DefaultAzureCredential`). For more information on authentication, see the [Azure Identity README](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md). ``` 2. Add a second example under `## Examples` for the `FromExisting` pattern (after the existing query-creation example): ```markdown ### Reference an Existing Resource Graph Query Use `FromExisting` to reference a shared query that is already deployed, so you can wire its outputs into other resources: ```C# Snippet:ResourceGraphQueryFromExisting ResourceGraphQuery existingQuery = ResourceGraphQuery.FromExisting(nameof(existingQuery)); existingQuery.Name = "my-existing-query"; infra.Add(existingQuery); ``` ``` 3. All rights reserved. // Licensed under the MIT License. using Azure.Provisioning.ResourceGraph; using NUnit.Framework; namespace Azure.Provisioning.ResourceGraph.Tests.Samples; public class SampleFromExisting { [Test] public void FromExistingExample() { #region Snippet:ResourceGraphQueryFromExisting Infrastructure infra = new(); ResourceGraphQuery existingQuery = ResourceGraphQuery.FromExisting(nameof(existingQuery)); existingQuery.Name = "my-existing-query"; infra.Add(existingQuery); #endregion } } ``` Add the following snippet placeholder in `README.md` under the new "Reference an Existing Resource Graph Query" sub-section (replace the manually-pasted code block shown in Step 1 above): ````markdown ```C# Snippet:ResourceGraphQueryFromExisting ``` ```` The `Update-Snippets.ps1` script will fill in the code automatically. ### Step 3: Verify README structure The README at `sdk/resourcegraph/Azure.Provisioning.ResourceGraph/README.md` is currently missing content in these sections: | Section | Status | Action | |---------|--------|--------| | Getting started > Authenticate the Client | ❌ Empty | Add content per Step 1 | | Key concepts | ✅ Present (brief, acceptable for initial beta) | No change needed | | Examples | ⚠️ One example only | Add `FromExisting` example per Step 1 | | Troubleshooting | ✅ Present | No change needed | | Next steps | ❌ Empty | Add links per Step 1 | | Contributing | ✅ Present | No change needed | Use `sdk/keyvault/Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets/README.md` as the canonical reference for section formatting and tone. ### Step 4: Validate Run these commands in order.
